points: On completion of the Experienced Worker assessment you will be able to take the end point assessment of competence, AM2E.

This qualification is for experienced electricians who can demonstrate over 5 years experience.
It is not suitable for new entrants or those currently on an apprenticeship.
syllabus.title: Completion & Next Steps

points: Our guidance on eligibility for the Experienced Worker Assessment (EWA) is based on the latest Electrotechnical Assessment Specification (EAS) Qualifications Guide, alongside advice from TESP and NET.
Some candidates may hold historic or legacy qualifications that are still accepted by certain industry schemes, even if they do not fully align with current EAS requirements.
Individual schemes may apply their own criteria and acceptance rules, which can differ from EAS guidance.

All advice we provide should be considered as guidance only. We cannot accept liability where a candidate undertakes an EWA and it is later determined that their existing qualifications were already accepted by an industry scheme.

Please note that industry schemes make the final decision on qualification acceptance. We strongly recommend that you check directly with your chosen registration body to confirm the status and acceptability of your qualifications.
syllabus.title: Important Guidance on Eligibility

qualifications.title: City & Guilds 2391-50, Initial Verification of Electrical Installations

points: The statutory and non-statutory documents governing electrical installations and inspection/testing work (e.g. national wiring regulations and relevant guidance).
Legal responsibilities, safe systems of work, safe isolation, risk assessment before inspection/testing on site.
Understanding of supply types (single-phase, three-phase), earthing/earthing systems, protective devices, and their implications for inspection and testing.
syllabus.title: Legislation, Standards & Safety

points: Visual inspection protocols and completion of pre-energisation checks before circuits are put into service.
Dead-testing procedures (continuity, polarity, insulation resistance).
Live-testing procedures where required (earth fault loop impedance, RCD and protective device verification, disconnection times, etc.).
Commissioning installation correctly; issuing of proper documentation including Electrical Installation Certificates (EIC) or Minor Works Certification where applicable.
syllabus.title: Initial Verification (New or Modified Installations)

points: Use and calibration of test instruments: insulation resistance testers, continuity testers, earth fault loop testers, RCD testers, multifunction testers. Proper procedures for connection, measurement and interpretation of results.
Fault finding and diagnosis – identifying wiring faults, earth/bonding issues, faulty protective devices, issues from wear and tear or poor installation.
Safe isolation and safe-working practices during testing and inspection; ensuring risks are managed appropriately before energising circuits.
syllabus.title: Practical Skills & Instrumentation

points: C1 (Danger Present) — Immediate risk requiring urgent action.

points: The Refrigeration Electrics Maintenance course covers the three elements which are common to most refrigeration and air conditioning systems: protection, control and motors.
Protection: fuses in fused connection units, circuit breakers protecting supply cables.
Control: thermostats, time clocks and other control devices.
Motors: motor driven fans and compressors.
During the course emphasis is placed upon the applicable wiring regulations to ensure the constructed system is compliant with BS7671:2022.
A typical air conditioning split system will be used as a practical example throughout the course.

description: Our Thermal Imaging Course is a one-day specialist training programme designed to introduce learners to the use of thermal imaging technology for inspection, diagnostics, and reporting.

Thermal imaging is widely used across industries as a safe, fast, and non-contact method of assessing equipment and systems. By detecting temperature variations, it allows professionals to identify faults, inefficiencies, and potential failures before they become serious issues.
This course provides both theoretical knowledge and practical skills required to effectively operate thermal imaging equipment and interpret results.

On successful completion of this course, you will have a strong, solid foundation for a career in industrial electrical maintenance.

points: Review of the Electricity at Work Act 1989 and relevant legal responsibilities for working electricians.
Safe working practices including isolation procedures and lock-out systems. Practical emphasis on safety.
Introduction to switches, push buttons: types, industry-standard symbols (BS symbols), and their correct application. Practical testing included.
Relays and contactors: principles of operation, intended uses in industrial circuits, and practical hands-on testing.
Circuit design fundamentals: use of ladder diagrams and BS electrical symbols to create simple control circuits.
Practical construction of circuits including correct cable termination techniques.
syllabus.title: Safety and Foundations

points: Study of three-phase induction motors (both synchronous and asynchronous). Understand internal components (stators, rotors), frame sizes, motor characteristics.
Demonstration of rotating magnetic fields and how a 3-phase system produces motion. Explanation of synchronous speed, slip, number of poles, and how these affect motor speed.
Practical recognition and identification of various motor types used in industry.
syllabus.title: Three-Phase Power and Motors

points: Introduction to fundamental motor control systems used in industrial settings.
Understanding overload protection devices and their correct sizing and application.
Design, assembly and wiring of a DOL starter circuit.
Testing and commissioning of the starter circuit, ensuring correct operation and safety.
syllabus.title: Motor Control Systems and Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starters

points: Modification of DOL starters to implement reversing starter control and power circuits.
Importance of accurate and up-to-date documentation whenever a circuit is modified.
Full construction and verification (power and control wiring) of a reversing motor starter circuit.
syllabus.title: Reversing Starter Circuits

points: Study of star-delta motor control starter systems: why star-delta starters are preferred in some industrial situations.
Design of star-delta control and power circuits.
Wiring, testing and verification of star-delta starter systems under real-world conditions.
syllabus.title: Star-Delta Starter Systems

points: Work with a variety of industrial components including solenoids, actuators, heaters, temperature controllers, thermal sensors. Learn their principles and safe handling.
Understand transformers: how they work, sizing, and use in industrial electrical systems.
syllabus.title: Industrial Components and Transformers

points: Introduction to proximity devices, limit switches and a variety of sensor types – how they operate and their typical applications.
Practical workshop wiring different sensor circuits, with attention to correct voltage supply and current type, and standard industry wiring practice.
Build simple control systems combining push buttons, sensors, relays, and indicator lamps to produce functional control logic.
syllabus.title: Sensors, Proximity Devices and Control Systems

points: Study of protective devices used in industrial electrical systems: overcurrent protection, fuses, circuit breakers, and their correct application and selection.
Understand principles of earthing, indirect shock protection, use of bonding and protective earths, and how to apply undervoltage protection safely.
Practical testing and verification of protective systems to ensure safety and compliance.
syllabus.title: Protection, Earthing and Safety Systems

points: Work with industrial-grade cables including types such as YY, SY, SWA and MICC. Learn proper stripping, crimping, soldering and termination methods.
Practical workshop terminating cables, then performing insulation resistance and continuity testing on terminated cables.
Use testing instruments correctly, interpret results, and recognise acceptable values for safe operation.
syllabus.title: Cable Work, Termination and Testing

points: Learn systematic fault-finding techniques: how to approach diagnosing electrical faults safely.
Hands-on work with fault simulation rigs to identify, isolate and correct real electrical faults under supervised conditions.
Root-cause analysis training to understand underlying causes of faults rather than superficial fixes.
Introduction to preventive maintenance (planned preventive maintenance, PPM) strategies to reduce downtime in industrial installations and extend lifespan of electrical systems.
syllabus.title: Advanced Fault-Finding, Maintenance, Root Cause Analysis and Preventive Maintenance

points: Review of the Electricity at Work Act 1989 and relevant legal responsibilities for working electricians.
Safe working practices including isolation procedures and lock-out systems. Practical emphasis on safety.
Introduction to switches, push buttons: types, industry-standard symbols (BS symbols), and their correct application. Practical testing included.
Relays and contactors: principles of operation, intended uses in industrial circuits, and practical hands-on testing.
Circuit design fundamentals: use of ladder diagrams and BS electrical symbols to create simple control circuits.
Practical construction of circuits including correct cable termination techniques.
syllabus.title: Safety and Foundations

points: Study of three-phase induction motors (both synchronous and asynchronous). Understand internal components (stators, rotors), frame sizes, motor characteristics.
Demonstration of rotating magnetic fields and how a 3-phase system produces motion. Explanation of synchronous speed, slip, number of poles, and how these affect motor speed.
Practical recognition and identification of various motor types used in industry.
syllabus.title: Three-Phase Power and Motors

points: Introduction to fundamental motor control systems used in industrial settings.
Understanding overload protection devices and their correct sizing and application.
Design, assembly and wiring of a DOL starter circuit.
Testing and commissioning of the starter circuit, ensuring correct operation and safety.
syllabus.title: Motor Control Systems and Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starters

points: Modification of DOL starters to implement reversing starter control and power circuits.
Importance of accurate and up-to-date documentation whenever a circuit is modified.
Full construction and verification (power and control wiring) of a reversing motor starter circuit.
syllabus.title: Reversing Starter Circuits

points: Study of star-delta motor control starter systems: why star-delta starters are preferred in some industrial situations.
Design of star-delta control and power circuits.
Wiring, testing and verification of star-delta starter systems under real-world conditions.
syllabus.title: Star-Delta Starter Systems

points: Work with a variety of industrial components including solenoids, actuators, heaters, temperature controllers, thermal sensors. Learn their principles and safe handling.
Understand transformers: how they work, sizing, and use in industrial electrical systems.
syllabus.title: Industrial Components and Transformers

points: Introduction to proximity devices, limit switches and a variety of sensor types – how they operate and their typical applications.
Practical workshop wiring different sensor circuits, with attention to correct voltage supply and current type, and standard industry wiring practice.
Build simple control systems combining push buttons, sensors, relays, and indicator lamps to produce functional control logic.
syllabus.title: Sensors, Proximity Devices and Control Systems

points: Study of protective devices used in industrial electrical systems: overcurrent protection, fuses, circuit breakers, and their correct application and selection.
Understand principles of earthing, indirect shock protection, use of bonding and protective earths, and how to apply undervoltage protection safely.
Practical testing and verification of protective systems to ensure safety and compliance.
syllabus.title: Protection, Earthing and Safety Systems

points: Work with industrial-grade cables including types such as YY, SY, SWA and MICC. Learn proper stripping, crimping, soldering and termination methods.
Practical workshop terminating cables, then performing insulation resistance and continuity testing on terminated cables.
Use testing instruments correctly, interpret results, and recognise acceptable values for safe operation.
syllabus.title: Cable Work, Termination and Testing

points: Learn systematic fault-finding techniques: how to approach diagnosing electrical faults safely.
Hands-on work with fault simulation rigs to identify, isolate and correct real electrical faults under supervised conditions.
Root-cause analysis training to understand underlying causes of faults rather than superficial fixes.
Introduction to preventive maintenance (PPM) strategies to reduce downtime in industrial installations and extend lifespan of electrical systems.
syllabus.title: Advanced Fault-Finding, Maintenance, Root Cause Analysis and Preventive Maintenance

points: A comprehensive Level 3 award combining both Initial Verification (for new or newly altered electrical installations) and Periodic Inspection & Testing (for existing installations).
Allows holders to certify, inspect, test and report on electrical installations in compliance with current UK standards.

children.text: The UK's air conditioning and refrigeration industry is booming. With rising demand for skilled technicians across commercial, industrial, and residential sectors, now is the perfect time to launch or advance your career in this essential trade.

Whether you're looking to change careers, upskill as an existing tradesperson, or train your workforce, the right air conditioning and refrigeration course can transform your earning potential and open doors to a stable, in-demand profession.
This guide breaks down everything you need to know about air conditioning and refrigeration training in the UK—from course types and certifications to what makes quality training stand out.
Why Train in Air Conditioning and Refrigeration?
Air conditioning and refrigeration technicians are essential across nearly every industry. From supermarkets and hospitals to offices and homes, cooling systems keep our world running smoothly.
Here's why this career path makes sense:
High demand: The UK faces a skills shortage in HVAC trades, meaning qualified technicians are highly sought after
Strong earning potential: Experienced AC and refrigeration engineers can earn £30,000–£55,000+ annually
Job security: Cooling systems require year-round installation, maintenance, and repair
Diverse work environments: Work in commercial refrigeration, domestic air conditioning, industrial cooling, or specialist sectors like healthcare and food storage
Career progression: Move into supervisory roles, start your own business, or specialise in advanced systems like heat pumps
The barrier to entry is lower than you think. You don't need prior electrical or technical experience to start—just the right training.
Types of Air Conditioning and Refrigeration Courses
Not all courses are created equal. Understanding the different training options helps you choose the right path for your goals.
1. Intensive Practical Courses (10–20 Days)

These fast-track courses are designed for career changers and tradespeople looking to add air conditioning and refrigeration skills quickly. They focus heavily on hands-on learning with real equipment.
Best for: Complete beginners with no technical background, electricians or plumbers expanding their skill set, or anyone wanting to enter the industry fast.

What you'll learn: Refrigeration cycle principles and thermodynamics, installing and commissioning AC and refrigeration systems, fault diagnosis and troubleshooting, electrical and mechanical components, and health and safety including safe refrigerant handling.
2. F-Gas Certification Courses
F-Gas certification is a legal requirement in the UK for anyone handling refrigerants. These courses range from Category I (leak checking) to Category II and III (installation and maintenance).
You'll learn safe handling and recovery of refrigerants, environmental regulations and compliance, and leak detection and system checks.
3. City & Guilds and Industry-Recognised Qualifications

Formal qualifications like City & Guilds 6187 or EAL diplomas provide nationally recognised credentials that employers trust. These are best for those seeking formal accredited qualifications, career changers who want recognised certification, or employers training staff to industry standards.
What to Look for in a Quality Training Provider
Hands-On Learning Over Theory

The best courses dedicate over 50% of training time to practical, hands-on work. You should be working with real equipment in true-to-life scenarios—not just watching demonstrations or reading manuals.
Individual Workstations with Real Equipment
Look for providers that give each student their own workstation with up-to-date, industry-standard equipment. Sharing equipment or working with outdated systems won't prepare you for real-world work.
Experienced Industry Tutors

Your tutors should have decades of actual industry experience—not just teaching credentials. The best instructors have worked in aviation, industrial refrigeration, commercial HVAC, or other demanding sectors and can share real-world insights.
Small Class Sizes
Personal attention matters. Small class sizes ensure you get individual support, can ask questions freely, and receive tailored feedback on your technique.
Accreditations and Certifications

Check that the provider is accredited by recognised bodies like City & Guilds, EAL, or BPEC. Quality providers also hold ISO certifications (ISO 9001, ISO 14001, ISO 45001) demonstrating commitment to training standards, environmental responsibility, and health and safety.
Common Questions About Air Conditioning and Refrigeration Training
Do I need prior experience?
No. Quality intensive courses are designed for complete beginners. If you can follow instructions and have a willingness to learn, you can succeed.
How long does it take to qualify?

Intensive practical courses range from 10 to 20 days, depending on the depth of training. F-Gas certification can be completed in as little as 5 days. Formal qualifications may take several weeks to months, depending on the level.
What certifications do I need to work in the UK?
At minimum, you'll need F-Gas certification to legally handle refrigerants. Many employers also look for City & Guilds or EAL qualifications, especially for installation and maintenance roles.
Can I start my own business after training?
Yes. With the right qualifications, F-Gas certification, and public liability insurance, you can work self-employed. Many technicians start by subcontracting before building their own client base.
What's the difference between air conditioning and refrigeration?

Both use the same refrigeration cycle principles, but air conditioning focuses on climate control in buildings, while refrigeration is used for food storage, industrial cooling, and commercial applications. Most courses cover both.
What You'll Learn in a Comprehensive Course
A quality air conditioning and refrigeration course should cover:
Refrigeration principles: Understanding the refrigeration cycle, thermodynamics, and heat transfer
System components: Compressors, condensers, evaporators, expansion valves, and controls
Installation skills: Pipework, brazing, electrical connections, and commissioning
Fault diagnosis: Identifying and fixing common issues like refrigerant leaks, electrical faults, and component failures
Maintenance procedures: Routine servicing, cleaning, and system optimisation
Health and safety: Safe working practices, refrigerant handling, and environmental compliance
Regulations and standards: F-Gas regulations, building regulations, and industry best practices
Career Paths After Qualifying
Once qualified, you can pursue a range of career paths:
Domestic air conditioning installer: Install and maintain AC systems in homes
Commercial refrigeration engineer: Service supermarket chillers, cold rooms, and display cabinets
Industrial HVAC technician: Work on large-scale cooling systems in factories, hospitals, or data centres
Heat pump specialist: Install and maintain renewable heating and cooling systems
Self-employed contractor: Build your own business serving residential or commercial clients
Supervisor or manager: Progress into team leadership or project management roles
Funding and Support Options
Training doesn't have to break the bank. Depending on your circumstances, you may be eligible for:
Government funding schemes: Apprenticeship Levy, Skills Bootcamps, or local authority grants
Employer-sponsored training: Many companies invest in upskilling their workforce
Flexible payment plans: Some providers offer instalment options to spread the cost
Always ask your training provider about available funding and support options.
Why Hands-On Training Matters

Here's the truth: you can't learn air conditioning and refrigeration from a textbook alone. This is a practical trade that requires muscle memory, problem-solving under pressure, and confidence working with live systems.
The best training gives you:
Real equipment: Not simulators or shared tools—individual workstations with the same systems you'll encounter on the job
True-to-life scenarios: Fault-finding exercises, installation challenges, and commissioning tasks that mirror real-world work
Immediate feedback: Experienced tutors who can correct your technique in real time and answer questions as they arise
Confidence: By the time you finish, you should feel ready to work independently—not like you need months of on-the-job training to catch up
Take the Next Step in Your Career

Air conditioning and refrigeration offer a clear path to a stable, well-paid career in a growing industry. Whether you're starting from scratch or adding new skills to your trade, the right training can get you there fast.

At Technique Learning Solutions, we specialise in intensive, hands-on courses designed to turn complete beginners into confident, job-ready technicians. With state-of-the-art facilities across the UK, individual workstations, and tutors with decades of real-world experience, we deliver training that actually prepares you for the job.

Our air conditioning and refrigeration courses combine practical skills with industry-recognised certifications, so you leave ready to work—not just ready to learn more. Explore our courses today and see why thousands of students trust us to transform their careers.
Your new career starts here.

children.text: The British Standard for fire detection and alarm systems, BS 5839, has undergone significant updates in 2025. Whether you're a practising electrician, fire alarm installer, or considering training in this essential field, understanding these changes is crucial for compliance and best practice.
What is BS 5839?

BS 5839 is the British Standard that provides recommendations and guidance for the design, installation, commissioning, and maintenance of fire detection and fire alarm systems in buildings. It's the industry benchmark that ensures fire safety systems are fit for purpose and can save lives.
Major Changes in the 2025 Update
1. Enhanced Requirements for Wireless Systems

The 2025 revision recognises the growing use of wireless fire alarm technology. New guidance includes stricter protocols for radio frequency interference testing, enhanced battery life monitoring requirements, mandatory redundancy measures for critical wireless links, and regular signal strength verification procedures.
2. Integration with Smart Building Technology

Reflecting modern construction practices, BS 5839:2025 now addresses compatibility requirements with Building Management Systems (BMS), cybersecurity considerations for networked fire alarm systems, data protection and privacy requirements for cloud-connected systems, and standards for remote monitoring and maintenance.
3. Updated Voice Alarm System Guidance

Significant updates to Part 8 (voice alarm systems) include improved intelligibility testing requirements, enhanced guidance on message prioritisation, new recommendations for multi-lingual emergency messaging, and updated acoustic performance standards in complex buildings.
4. Environmental Sustainability

For the first time, BS 5839:2025 incorporates environmental considerations including guidance on energy-efficient system design, recommendations for recyclable components, end-of-life disposal requirements, and reduced false alarm strategies to minimise unnecessary emergency responses.
5. False Alarm Management

Enhanced focus on reducing unwanted fire signals includes more detailed guidance on detector selection for specific environments, improved commissioning procedures to prevent false alarms, requirements for cause and effect analysis, and mandatory false alarm logs and review procedures.
6. Domestic and Residential Care Settings

Updated recommendations cover Grade D and F systems in domestic properties, enhanced guidance for care homes and supported living facilities, interconnection requirements for multi-occupancy buildings, and specific provisions for residents with hearing or mobility impairments.
What This Means for Electricians and Installers
Training and Competence

The updated standard emphasises the importance of proper training. Installers must demonstrate competence in new wireless technologies and their limitations, cybersecurity basics for networked systems, advanced commissioning procedures, and false alarm reduction strategies.
Documentation Requirements

More comprehensive documentation is now required, including detailed as-fitted drawings with wireless signal strength maps, cybersecurity risk assessments for connected systems, enhanced commissioning certificates, and comprehensive user training records.
Transition Period and Compliance

While the 2025 standard is now published, there's typically a transition period during which both old and new standards are acceptable. New installations should comply with BS 5839:2025 immediately, existing systems should be upgraded where reasonably practicable, and all maintenance and modifications should reference the latest standard.
Training for BS 5839:2025 Compliance

Staying current with these changes is essential for anyone working in fire alarm installation and maintenance. At Technique Training, our fire alarm courses are continuously updated to reflect the latest standards and industry best practices. Our experienced instructors bring real-world knowledge from years in the field, and our hands-on approach ensures you're confident working with modern fire detection systems.
Key Takeaways

BS 5839:2025 brings significant updates reflecting modern technology and building practices. Wireless systems, smart building integration, and cybersecurity are major focus areas. False alarm reduction and environmental sustainability receive enhanced attention. Proper training and documentation are more important than ever. Staying compliant protects lives, property, and your professional reputation.

children.text: The demand for skilled air conditioning engineers has never been higher. With climate change driving increased reliance on cooling systems and strict environmental regulations requiring qualified professionals, now is the perfect time to train in this rewarding field.

Whether you're considering a complete career change or you're an existing tradesperson looking to add air conditioning to your skill set, this guide covers everything you need to know about air conditioning training in the UK.
Why Train as an Air Conditioning Engineer?
Growing Industry Demand

The HVAC sector is experiencing significant growth across residential, commercial, and industrial settings. Businesses and homeowners alike need qualified engineers to install, maintain, and repair increasingly sophisticated air conditioning systems.
Excellent Career Prospects
Qualified air conditioning engineers enjoy:
Strong earning potential with competitive salaries and self-employment opportunities
Job security in an essential, recession-resistant industry
Diverse work environments from offices and retail to hospitals and data centres
Continuous learning with evolving technology and environmental regulations
No Degree Required
You don't need a university degree to become an air conditioning engineer. Practical, hands-on training and industry-recognized qualifications are what employers value most.
What Does Air Conditioning Training Cover?
Core Skills and Knowledge
Professional air conditioning training typically includes:
System fundamentals: Understanding refrigeration cycles, heat transfer, and cooling principles
Installation techniques: Proper mounting, pipework, electrical connections, and commissioning
Maintenance procedures: Routine servicing, filter changes, and preventative care
Fault diagnosis: Identifying and resolving common system problems
Safety protocols: Working safely with refrigerants, electrical systems, and pressure vessels
Environmental compliance: F-Gas regulations and proper refrigerant handling
Customer service: Professional communication and site management
Hands-On Practical Training

The most effective air conditioning courses emphasize practical experience. At Technique Training, over 50% of course time is spent working with real equipment at individual workstations—not watching demonstrations or working in large groups.
You'll work with:
State-of-the-art air conditioning units from multiple manufacturers
Real-world installation scenarios
Diagnostic tools and testing equipment
Live electrical systems with robust safety protocols
This hands-on approach ensures you're job-ready from day one, not just theoretically knowledgeable.
Who Should Consider Air Conditioning Training?
Career Changers

Many successful air conditioning engineers come from completely different backgrounds. If you're looking for a practical, well-paid career with excellent prospects, air conditioning offers a genuine opportunity regardless of your previous experience.
Our students average 38 years old, with many in their 30s and 40s making successful career transitions. Employers value the maturity, professionalism, and work ethic that career changers bring.
Existing Tradespeople
If you're already working as an electrician, plumber, or general maintenance professional, adding air conditioning qualifications can significantly expand your service offering and income potential.
Cross-training allows you to:
Take on more comprehensive projects
Increase your day rate
Differentiate yourself from competitors
Build a more resilient business
Company Training Programs

Manufacturers, facilities management companies, and engineering firms regularly send staff for air conditioning training to meet compliance requirements, expand service capabilities, improve staff retention, and maintain competitive advantage.
What Qualifications Do You Need?
Industry-Recognized Certifications
Professional air conditioning training leads to qualifications from respected awarding bodies including:
City & Guilds
EAL (Excellence, Achievement & Learning)
Logic Certification
BPEC (Building Performance Engineering Competence)
F-Gas Certification

Working with refrigerants requires F-Gas certification, which demonstrates your competence in handling refrigerants safely and in compliance with environmental regulations. This is a legal requirement for anyone working with air conditioning systems.
How Long Does Training Take?
Air conditioning courses vary in length depending on the level and specialization:
Foundation courses: Typically 5-10 days for comprehensive introduction
Specialist modules: 3-5 days for specific skills like fault finding or advanced systems
Integrated programs: Several weeks for complete qualification packages
At Technique Training, we offer flexible scheduling including evening and weekend options, designed specifically for working professionals who can't take extended time off.
What Makes Quality Air Conditioning Training?
Small Class Sizes

Personal attention matters. With class sizes of 1-10 students, you receive individual guidance and can ask questions without competing for the instructor's time. Unlike large training providers, we never cancel courses due to low enrollment.
Experienced Instructors

Your tutors should have extensive real-world industry experience, not just teaching credentials. Our instructors bring combined decades of experience from aviation, industrial, and commercial HVAC sectors.
Real Equipment, Not Simulators

Training on actual air conditioning units—not computer simulations—prepares you for real job sites. You should work with equipment from multiple manufacturers to gain brand-neutral, transferable skills.
Career Paths After Training
Employment Opportunities
Qualified air conditioning engineers find work in:
HVAC installation and maintenance companies
Facilities management organizations
Manufacturing and industrial plants
Commercial property management
Specialist refrigeration firms
Self-employment and contracting
Typical Career Progression

Many graduates secure employment during or immediately after their training. Most new air conditioning engineers either join established HVAC companies as employed engineers, start self-employed contracting businesses, or add air conditioning services to existing trade businesses.
Continued Professional Development

The HVAC industry constantly evolves with new technologies, refrigerants, and regulations. Advanced and specialist courses allow you to specialize in commercial or industrial systems, master advanced fault diagnosis, learn emerging technologies like heat pumps, and stay current with regulatory changes.
Why Choose Technique Training?
Since 2011, Technique Training has been the UK's leading provider of electrical, air conditioning, refrigeration, and automation training. Here's what sets us apart:
Unmatched Practical Focus: Over 50% of course time is hands-on work with real equipment at individual workstations.
Brand-Neutral Training: We use equipment from multiple manufacturers, ensuring transferable skills.
Industry Connections: We partner with major employers across the HVAC sector and provide job placement assistance.
Never-Cancelled Classes: With class sizes of 1-10 students, we never cancel due to low enrollment.
ISO Accredited Excellence: We hold ISO 9001, 14001, and 45001 accreditations.
State-of-the-Art Facilities: Our flagship centre in Chesterfield Clay Cross spans 180,000 square feet, with additional centres in Oxford and Stirling.
98% First-Time Pass Rate: Our focused, practical approach delivers results.
Getting Started
Next Steps
Ready to start your air conditioning training journey? Here's what to do:
Research courses: Identify which qualifications match your goals and experience level
Contact training providers: Ask about course content, schedules, and costs
Book early: Advance booking allows for better planning and customization
Arrange logistics: Consider accommodation, transport, and time off work if needed
Conclusion

Air conditioning training opens doors to a rewarding, well-paid career in an essential industry. With the right training provider, you can gain the practical skills, industry-recognized qualifications, and employer connections needed to succeed—whether you're changing careers entirely or expanding your existing trade skills.
The combination of growing demand, excellent earning potential, and genuine job security makes air conditioning engineering one of the smartest career choices for 2026 and beyond.
Ready to take the next step? Contact Technique Training to discuss your air conditioning training options and start your journey toward a rewarding new career.
